---
title: "TOPIC NAME"
---

<!--
Copy this file to topics/<your-topic>.qmd and fill in each section.
Keep the two-part shape every chapter in this book uses:
  1. Minimal analysis  — smallest defensible code, common packages.
  2. Simulation evidence — recover a KNOWN truth; report bias & coverage.
Delete these comments before submitting your pull request.
-->

## What a reviewer wants

<!-- 2-4 sentences: what is the estimand / quantity of interest, and why the
     minimal common-package approach below is the sensible default. Name the
     package(s) you use and why they are the common, trusted choice. -->

## Minimal analysis

```{r}
#| label: minimal
#| message: false
# The smallest amount of readable code that produces the estimate a reviewer
# cares about. Prefer base R; add common packages only when they earn their
# place. Show the resulting estimate + CI.
```

<!-- One short paragraph walking through what the code does. -->

## Simulation evidence: does the method recover the truth?

```{r}
#| label: sim-setup
source("R/sim_utils.R")  # project root is the working dir (see _quarto.yml)

TRUE_VALUE <- NA   # the parameter your estimator targets
N_REP      <- 2000

gen <- function(i) {
  # Return one simulated data set drawn from a process where TRUE_VALUE holds.
}

fit <- function(d) {
  # Fit the MINIMAL method above and return:
  #   list(estimate=, se=, lower=?, upper=?)
}
```

```{r}
#| label: sim-run
res <- run_simulation(N_REP, gen, fit, seed = 1)
print_eval(evaluate_estimator(res$estimate, res$se, TRUE_VALUE,
                              res$lower, res$upper))
```

<!-- Interpret the row: bias near 0? se_ratio near 1? coverage near 0.95?
     If the estimator is biased or under-covers, SAY SO — a documented caveat
     is more valuable than a hidden one. -->

## Takeaways

<!-- 2-3 bullets: the recommended minimal code, and what the simulation showed. -->

```{r}
#| label: session
sessionInfo()
```
